summary: Hotz argues that nuclear weapons, like Chekhov's gun, will inevitably be used at some point in human history and questions whether delaying their use is actually preferable. He contends that future weapons (drone swarms, bioweapons) will be far more horrific than nukes, making today's nuclear fears seem quaint in retrospect. He suggests nuclear war wouldn't end humanity—comparing casualties to the Black Death—and might even create more robust, decentralized systems. The post concludes with an accelerationist provocation: if nuclear war is inevitable, perhaps sooner is better than later.
key_points: Nuclear weapons are Chekhov's gun—once introduced to the world stage, they will eventually be fired
Future weapons like targeted bioweapons and drone swarms will make nukes seem primitive and 'quaint'
Nuclear war would kill roughly a third of humanity but wouldn't cause extinction or permanent nuclear winter
Post-nuclear systems would be forced to decentralize and become more robust
Accelerationist logic suggests if something is inevitable, it's better to get it over with sooner
money_quotes: Nuclear weapons are the Chekhov's gun on the world stage.
I suspect that in 100 years nuclear weapons will seem quaint.
Oh that's cute, it blows up a city. This new weapon seeks out and kills every <insert race here> person on earth and tortures them before they die.
Nobody wants an irradiated pile of rubble.
I understand the loss of short-term growth is a hard pill to swallow, but would we be better or worse off in 100 years if we fired all the nukes today?
The nukes will force systems to decentralize and become less complex, but in exchange those systems will become more robust.
Do you really think the nukes won't be used for the rest of human history?
Once the gun has been placed on the stage, it will be fired.
key_insight: If nuclear war is historically inevitable, an accelerationist might argue that getting it over with sooner could leave humanity better positioned for the long term.
